Most Angular developers use shareReplay() incorrectly. Learn when it leaks memory, why it happens, and the patterns you should use instead.

Introduction
A few months ago, I reviewed an Angular application that had become noticeably slower after several hours of use.
There were no obvious errors. CPU usage looked fine. Network requests were normal.
The real culprit?
One line of RxJS code:
shareReplay(1)It appeared in dozens of services.
Many developers believe shareReplay() is simply a caching operator. In reality, using it incorrectly can keep subscriptions alive far longer than intended, leading to memory leaks and stale data.
Let’s see why.

What is shareReplay()?
Explain:

• multicasting

• caching

• replaying previous values
Example:
this.users$ = this.http.get('/api/users').pipe(
shareReplay(1)
);Benefits:

• Prevents duplicate HTTP requests

• Shares a single subscription

• Replays the latest value to new subscribers

The Hidden Problem
Consider:
shareReplay(1)Many developers assume the subscription ends automatically.
It doesn’t.
Internally:
Component
↓
Observable
↓
shareReplay()
↓
Source Observable
↓
Subscription stays aliveThe source may never unsubscribe.

Memory Leak Example
@Injectable()
export class UserService {users$ = interval(1000).pipe(
shareReplay(1)
);
}
Every second:
0
1
2
3
4
...Even if no component is listening anymore, the interval continues.
That’s a memory leak.

The Correct Way
Use:
shareReplay({
bufferSize:1,
refCount:true
})Now the observable automatically disconnects when there are no subscribers.

HTTP Requests vs Long-lived Streams
HTTP
Good
http.get(...).pipe(
shareReplay(1)
)Why?
HTTP completes automatically.
No leak.

WebSocket
Bad
websocket.pipe(
shareReplay(1)
)Needs:
shareReplay({
bufferSize:1,
refCount:true
})
interval()
Definitely use
refCount:true
Angular Signals
If you’re using Angular Signals:
toSignal(
this.users$
)Even with Signals, the Observable lifecycle still matters.
Signals don’t automatically fix an incorrectly shared observable.

Common Mistakes
❌ Using shareReplay(1) everywhere
❌ Caching WebSocket streams
❌ Caching intervals
❌ Ignoring subscription lifecycles
❌ Assuming Angular destroys the stream automatically

Best Practices
✅ Use refCount:true for long-lived streams.
✅ Cache HTTP requests safely.
✅ Prefer Signals for component state.
✅ Use DevTools to inspect subscriptions.
✅ Test services that expose shared observables.

Key Takeaways

• shareReplay() is powerful but easy to misuse.

• HTTP requests are generally safe because they complete.

• Long-lived streams such as interval() and WebSockets need careful lifecycle management.

• refCount: true often prevents subscriptions from lingering after the last subscriber disconnects.
